coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots/β-cyclodextrin nanocomposites: A novel electrochemical chiral interface for tryptophan isomer recognition
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Ou, Jie
Zhu, Yuhang
Kong, Yong
Ma, Jianfeng
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared by pyrolysis of citric acid, and then incorporated into β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) via H-bonds between the oxygen-containing groups on GQDs and the hydroxyl groups on β-CD. The nanocomposites of GQDs and β-CD (GQDs/β-CD) were negatively charged due to the ionization of carboxyl groups of GQDs, and therefore they could be effectively electrodeposited onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The electrodeposited GQDs/β-CD were optically active due to the introduction of β-CDs with well-defined hydrophobic central cavities, and it was employed as an electrochemical chiral interface for enantiorecognition of tryptophan (Trp) isomers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
β-Cyclodextrin
Chiral interface
Tryptophan isomers
Electrochemical recognition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical characteristics of GQDs/β-CD modified GCE
Electrochemical chiral recognition of Trp isomers
Influence of temperature and pH on chiral recognition

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrating the preparation of GQDs/β-CD and the recognition of Trp isomers by the nanocomposites.

(A) TEM image of GQDs. Inset: size distribution histograms. (B) FT-IR spectra of GQDs/β-CD, GQDs and β-CD. (C) Cyclic voltammograms of bare GCE, GQDs/β-CD, GQDs and β-CD-modified GCE in 0.1M KCl containing 10mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4−. (D) Nyquist plots of GCE, GQDs/β-CD, GQDs and β-CD modified GCE in 0.1M KCl containing 10mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. Inset: equivalent circuit.
Differential pulse voltammograms of l-Trp and d-Trp included with GQDs/β-CD (A) and β-CD (B) modified GCE in 0.1M PBS (pH7.0) at 15°C. SEM images of pure β-CD (C) and GQDs/β-CD (D) modified GCE.

Influence of temperature (A) and pH (B) on the enantiorecognition efficiency of the GQDs/β-CD-modified GCE toward Trp isomers. Errors bars represent the standard deviation for three independent measurements.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots/β-cyclodextrin nanocomposites: A novel electrochemical chiral interface for tryptophan isomer recognition

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology, School of Petrochemical Engineering, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared by pyrolysis of citric acid, and then incorporated into β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) via H-bonds between the oxygen-containing groups on GQDs and the hydroxyl groups on β-CD. The nanocomposites of GQDs and β-CD (GQDs/β-CD) were negatively charged due to the ionization of carboxyl groups of GQDs, and therefore they could be effectively electrodeposited onto a glassy carbon electrode (GCE). The electrodeposited GQDs/β-CD were optically active due to the introduction of β-CDs with well-defined hydrophobic central cavities, and it was employed as an electrochemical chiral interface for enantiorecognition of tryptophan (Trp) isomers.

A novel chiral interface based on the GQDs/β-CD nanocomposites is constructed for electrochemical recognition of Trp enantiomers, which combines the advantages of GQDs (highly negatively charged) and β-CD (chiral microenvironment), and therefore they are applied for the successful recognition of Trp isomers.

ce:section.ce:para: In supramolecular systems, β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) has emerged as an ideal host material to accommodate various guest molecules due to its truncated cone-shaped and hydrophobic central cavity [1], and therefore it has been widely employed for enantiorecognition of various isomers [2,3]. However, little attention has been paid to β-CD-based electrochemical chiral recognition, probably due to the inferior conductivity of β-CD. Recently, electrochemical recognition of tryptophan (Trp) isomers has been reported by our group by combining β-CD or copper-modified β-CD with poly(l-glutamic acid) [4,5], suggesting the possibility of β-CD-based electrochemical enantiorecognition.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are graphene sheets smaller than 100nm [6], possessing similar properties to graphene. The unique quantum confinement and edge effect of GQDs have drawn the attention of researchers from various fields. However, there are few reports on GQD-based enantiorecognition owing to the lack of chiral sites in GQDs. Recently, Trojanowicz [7] reported that the combination of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and optically active substances can achieve recognition of chiral compounds, suggesting the feasibility of GQDs-based enantiorecognition because both GQDs and CNTs are carbon nanomaterials. Another recent work by our group also verified the possibility of GQDs-based chiral recognition, in which Trp isomers were recognized by the nanocomposites of GQDs and chitosan, a natural polysaccharide having plenty of chiral centers [8].

Herein, we first report an electrochemical chiral interface for the recognition of Trp isomers by the nanocomposites of GQDs and β-CD (GQDs/β-CD) formed through H-bonds. The GQDs/β-CD are negatively charged due to the ionization of the carboxyl groups of GQDs, and they can be effectively electrodeposited onto the surface of glassy carbon electrodes (GCE); meanwhile, the nanocomposites of GQDs/β-CD are capable of recognizing Trp isomers due to the introduction of β-CD possessing a hydrophobic central cavity.

All chemicals were analytical grade and used as received. The electrochemical measurements were performed on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation in a three-electrode system using the GQDs/β-CD electrodeposited GCE as the working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference, and a platinum foil as the auxiliary electrode, respectively. The FT-IR spectra were measured on a FTIR-8400S spectrometer, and TEM and SEM images were recorded on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope and a Supra55 field emission scanning electron microscope, respectively.

GQDs were prepared based on the method previously reported using citric acid as a precursor [9], and then the GQD powder was dissolved in water to form a yellow solution (2mgmL−1). Next, 113.5mg of β-CD was added to 10mL of GQD solution, and the GQD/β-CD nanocomposites were obtained through the formation of H-bonds between GQDs and β-CD. Finally, homogenous GQD/β-CD films were electrodeposited onto GCE (3mm diameter) by cyclic voltammetry (CV) in the potential range from 0 to 1.0V at a scan rate of 100mVs−1 for 50cycles.

The GQD/β-CD modified GCE was immersed into 20mL of 0.1M PBS containing 0.5mM l-Trp or d-Trp (pH7.0) for 60s at 15°C, and then the differential pulse voltammograms (DPVs) of the two formed inclusion complexes, GQDs/β-CD/l-Trp and GQDs/β-CD/d-Trp, were recorded. The recognition efficiency was evaluated by monitoring the differences in oxidation peak current (I p) in the two voltammograms. Due to the preferential inclusion complexation between β-CD and l-Trp, l-Trp exhibits higher I p at the GQD/β-CD modified GCE. The schematic illustrating the preparation of GQDs/β-CD and the recognition of Trp isomers by the nanocomposites is shown in Fig. 1 .

Fig. 2A shows the TEM image of GQDs prepared by the pyrolysis of citric acid. GQDs are fairly well monodispersed and the size distribution of the GQDs estimated from TEM has an average diameter of 2.4nm. After β-CD is added to the GQD solution, intermolecular H-bonds are formed between hydroxyl groups of β-CD and oxygen-containing groups of GQDs, which is verified by the FT-IR spectra of GQDs/β-CD, GQDs and β-CD (Fig. 2B). For pure β-CD, the characteristic peak at 1029cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of C—O—C. The two peaks of GQDs at 1720 and 1635cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of C O and C C, respectively. All characteristic peaks of β-CD and GQDs can still be observed in the spectra of GQDs/β-CD, indicating that β-CD is successfully attached to GQDs. It is noteworthy that the stretching of C O and C C at GQDs are shifted to 1716 and 1637cm−1, respectively, at the GQDs/β-CD, which could be ascribed to the formation of H-bonds between β-CD and GQDs [10,11].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characteristics of GQDs/β-CD modified GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical characterizations of the GQDs/β-CD also indicate the successful combination of GQDs and β-CD. Fig. 2C shows the cyclic voltammograms of different electrodes in 0.1M KCl containing 10mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4−. As can be seen, the redox couple of Fe(CN)6 3−/4− exhibits a pair of well-defined redox peaks at bare GCE. After β-CD is modified to the GCE by CV, the I p in the voltammogram decreases a little because of the inferior conductivity of β-CD. An obvious decrease in the I p is found at the GQDs-modified GCE prepared by the same procedure as that of GQDs/β-CD, which could be ascribed to the electrostatic repulsion between negatively charged Fe(CN)6 3−/4− and the —COO− groups on GQDs resulted from the ionization of carboxyl groups [12,13]. The I p is further decreased at the GQDs and β-CD co-deposited GCE due to the synergistic effect of GQDs and β-CD. The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) exhibit the same change tendency as that of the cyclic voltammograms (Fig. 2D), and the largest charge transfer resistance (R ct) is observed at the GQDs/β-CD-modified GCE, suggesting that the resistance at the electrode–solution interface is much more significant at the GQDs/β-CD-modified GCE than that at pure GQDs and pure β-CD-modified GCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical chiral recognition of Trp isomers
ce:section.ce:para: The recognition ability of the GQDs/β-CD-based chiral interface is evaluated by DPVs (Fig. 3A). As can be seen, the inclusion complex of GQDs/β-CD/l-Trp generates a significantly higher peak current than GQDs/β-CD/d-Trp, and the peak current ratio of GQDs/β-CD/l-Trp to GQDs/β-CD/d-Trp (I L/I D) is calculated to be 2.10. This result indicates that compared with d-Trp, β-CD preferably includes with l-Trp owing to the favorability of H-bond formation between the secondary hydroxyl groups on β-CD rims and the amino group of l-Trp when the indole group of Trp isomers inserts into the cavity of β-CD [4]. It is interesting to find that although tapered hydrophobic cavities in β-CD that are capable of including various guest molecules exist, the enantiorecognition efficiency (I L/I D) at pure β-CD-modified GCE is dramatically inferior to that of the GQDs/β-CD-modified GCE (1.02 versus 2.10; Fig. 3B). The deteriorated recognition efficiency at pure β-CD-modified GCE may be ascribed to the fact that due to the dielectric and insulating features of β-CD, β-CD is attached to the surface of GCE by adsorption but not electrostatic attraction. As a result, the amount of β-CD deposited onto GCE by CV is too small to effectively recognize the Trp isomers. This assumption is further verified by the SEM images of pure β-CD and GQDs/β-CD-modified GCE. As can be seen, a random and irregular distribution of pure β-CD on GCE is observed, and a majority of the GCE surface is still uncovered by β-CD (Fig. 3C). However, it is quite different for GQDs/β-CD-modified GCE, since a compact and homogeneous petal-shaped GQDs/β-CD film is formed at the surface of GCE (Fig. 3D). The surfaces of GQDs/β-CD are highly negatively charged when dispersed in water, apparently as a result of ionization of the carboxyl groups on the GQDs. Therefore, a strong electrostatic attraction occurs between negatively charged GQDs/β-CD and the surface of GCE when CV over the potential range from 0 to 1.0V is exerted on the GCE, leading to a significant enhancement in the electrodeposited amount of enantioselective β-CD on the GCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of temperature and pH on chiral recognition
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, the influences of temperature and pH on the chiral recognition of Trp isomers are investigated. Since the strength of H-bonds is significantly dependent on temperature, there is no doubt that temperature plays an important role in the formation of H-bonds in the GQDs/β-CD nanocomposites and the subsequent enantiorecognition. It is observed that the highest recognition efficiency is obtained at 15°C (Fig. 4A). At low temperatures ranging from 0°C to 10°C, the H-bonds strength is relatively weaker, resulting in a decrease in the recognition efficiency. However, an excessively high temperature, especially from 25°C to 30°C, may lead to the breakage of stable H-bonds within the nanocomposites and the subsequent decrease in recognition efficiency.

The pH of the PBS for the inclusion complexation of GQDs and β-CD also has a great effect on chiral recognition, as can be seen from Fig. 4B. The maximum I p ratio of Trp isomers at the GQDs/β-CD modified GCE is found at pH7.0. Increased acidity will result in the decomposition of natural polysaccharide including β-CD and excessively high alkalinity can increase the solubility of GQDs in water, so both acidic and alkaline environments are disadvantageous to the enantiorecognition of Trp isomers by the GQDs/β-CD nanocomposites. As a result, a moderate pH value of 7.0 is chosen for the inclusion complexation of GQDs and β-CD.

A novel chiral interface based on the GQDs/β-CD nanocomposites is proposed for electrochemical enantiorecognition of Trp isomers. GQDs are combined with β-CD via H-bonds between the oxygen-containing groups on GQDs and the hydroxyl groups on β-CD. By CV, compact and homogeneous GQDs/β-CD film is electrodeposited on the surface GCE owing to the electrostatic interactions. The GQDs/β-CD nanocomposites combine the advantages of GQDs (highly negatively charged) and β-CD (chiral microenvironment), and therefore they are applied for the recognition of Trp isomers. This work provides a beneficial reference for the construction of novel electrochemical chiral interfaces based on the nanocomposites of GQDs and natural polysaccharide via a simple and convenient approach.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21275023), Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology (BM2012110) and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).
